title: Suricata TLS Client JA3 Fingerprints Rare in the Enterprise (Possible Custom Malware C2)
dataSources: Suricata EVE JSON TLS events, commonly `sourcetype=suricata:tls` when using the Splunk TA for Suricata
spl:
index=ids sourcetype="suricata:tls" earliest=-7d
| eval ja3=coalesce(ja3, ja3_hash, tls_ja3)
| eval src_ip=coalesce(src_ip, src)
| eval dest_ip=coalesce(dest_ip, dest)
| where isnotnull(ja3) AND ja3!=""
| eventstats dc(src_ip) as ja3_src_rarity by ja3
| where ja3_src_rarity <= 5
| stats dc(dest_ip) as unique_servers count as handshakes values(server_name) as sni_samples by src_ip, ja3
| where handshakes >= 12 AND unique_servers >= 6
| sort -handshakes
description: Most enterprise clients present a small set of JA3 hashes from standard browsers and patch levels. A source IP generating many connections with a rarely seen JA3 while contacting diverse destinations may indicate custom malware, red-team tooling, or outdated TLS stacks being abused.
value: Adds behaviour-based coverage on encrypted channels where payload inspection is not available.
implementation: Confirm Suricata outputs `tls` events in EVE JSON and that the TA maps them to `suricata:tls`. Tune `ja3_hits` using a 7-day window on your own data. Enrich `src_ip` with DHCP or AD identity. Exclude known vulnerability scanners and patch-management relays via lookup.
visualization: Table (src_ip, JA3, connection count), Hunt timeline, Pair with `suricata:flow` for byte ratios.
knownFalsePositives: Rare TLS fingerprint patterns can be custom apps, new browsers, or TLS middleboxes, not just malware — compare to change-managed releases.
grandmaExplanation: We use rare TLS handshakes on the wire to spotlight custom or odd clients that can hide a private channel in normal traffic.
